lessons.title: Everyday Greetings and Introductions
lessons.description: Learn how to greet people and introduce yourself in English

vocabulary.definition: A greeting used to say hi to someone
A polite response when meeting someone for the first time
vocabulary.example: Nice to meet you! I'm Sarah.

vocabulary.definition: A common question to ask about someone's wellbeing

dialogue.speaker: Person B
dialogue.text: My name is Anna. Nice to meet you!

dialogue.speaker: Person A
dialogue.text: Nice to meet you too! How are you?

dialogue.speaker: Person B
dialogue.text: I'm fine, thank you. And you?

lessons.title: Shopping at the Market
lessons.description: Learn vocabulary and phrases for shopping

vocabulary.definition: A question to ask the price
Costs more money than you want to pay
vocabulary.example: This jacket is too expensive for me.
Can I help you find something?

dialogue.speaker: Customer
dialogue.text: That's a bit expensive. Do you have anything cheaper?

dialogue.speaker: Shopkeeper
dialogue.text: Yes, we have this one for £1.50.

lessons.title: Discussing Past Experiences
lessons.description: Learn to talk about what you did using past tense

vocabulary.example: Yesterday I went to the cinema.

examples: I went to Paris last year.
We had a great time at the beach.

dialogue.speaker: Friend B
dialogue.text: I went to the beach with my family.

dialogue.speaker: Friend B
dialogue.text: It was amazing! The weather was perfect and the water was warm.

lessons.title: Academic Writing: Argumentative Essays
lessons.description: Learn to structure and write persuasive arguments

vocabulary.definition: Additionally; also used in academic writing
vocabulary.example: Furthermore, this approach has proven effective.
A common counterargument is that technology reduces human interaction.
Nonetheless, we must consider the evidence.

grammar.rule: Connecting ideas with conjunctions and discourse markers
examples: Although some argue..., the evidence suggests...
While it is true that..., nonetheless...

lessons.title: Ordering Food at a Restaurant
lessons.description: Learn common phrases for ordering food and making requests

vocabulary.example: I have a reservation for a table for two.

vocabulary.definition: A particular item offered at a reduced price or as a limited offer
To suggest as being good or suitable

examples: Could I have the menu, please?
Would you like something to drink?

dialogue.speaker: Waiter
dialogue.text: Good evening! Welcome to our restaurant. Do you have a reservation?

dialogue.speaker: Customer
dialogue.text: Yes, I have a reservation for two under the name Smith.

dialogue.speaker: Waiter
dialogue.text: Perfect! Here's your table. What would you like to drink?
